message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>BACKGROUND</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) has recently become a public emergency and a worldwide pandemic. The clinical symptoms of severe and non-severe patients vary, and the case-fatality rate (CFR) in severe COVID-19 patients is very high. However, the information on the risk factors associated with the severity of COVID-19 and of their prognostic potential is limited.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>METHODS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this retrospective study, the clinical characteristics, laboratory findings, treatment and outcome data were collected and analyzed from 223 COVID-19 patients stratified into 125 non-severe patients and 98 severe patients. In addition, a pooled large-scale meta-analysis of 1646 cases was performed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We found that the age, gender and comorbidities are the common risk factors associated with the severity of COVID-19. For the diagnosis markers, we found that the levels of D-dimer, C-reactive protein (CRP), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), procalcitonin (PCT) were significantly higher in severe group compared with the non-severe group on admission (D-Dimer: 87.3% vs. 35.3%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001; CRP, 65.1% vs. 13.5%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001; LDH: 83.9% vs. 22.2%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001; PCT: 35.1% vs. 2.2%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001), while the levels of aspartate aminotransferase (ASP) and creatinine kinase (CK) were only mildly increased. We also made a large scale meta-analysis of 1646 cases combined with 4 related literatures, and further confirmed the relationship between the COVID-19 severity and these risk factors. Moreover, we tracked dynamic changes during the process of COVID-19, and found CRP, D-dimer, LDH, PCT kept in high levels in severe patient. Among all these markers, D-dimer increased remarkably in severe patients and mostly related with the case-fatality rate (CFR). We found adjuvant antithrombotic treatment in some severe patients achieved good therapeutic effect in the cohort.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The diagnosis markers CRP, D-dimer, LDH and PCT are associated with severity of COVID-19. Among these markers, D-dimer is sensitive for both severity and CFR of COVID-19. Treatment with heparin or other anticoagulants may be beneficial for COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study was supported by funding from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFC1302203); Beijing Nova Program (grant number: xx2018040).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Role of the funding source</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The funding listed above supports this study, but had no role in the design and conduct of the study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: A cohort study of 223 patients explores the clinical risk factors for the severity diagnosis of COVID-19

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization. Novel Coronavirus (COVID-19) Situation. https://www.who.int/redirect-pages/page/novel-coronavirus-(covid-19)-situation-dashboard. Accessed April 18th, 2020.
